title: Anger and Irritability
welcomeMessage: Learn to control anger and irritation. Master emotional regulation techniques for a calmer life.

cards.introduction:
Anger rarely comes “out of nowhere”. Most of the time it’s a signal: a boundary got crossed, something felt unfair, you lost control, you’re exhausted.

Many men hold it in to look strong—then it comes out as a blow‑up. Anger itself doesn’t make you a bad person.

The key is to understand what triggers it and where it builds. In this card you’ll map your triggers so you can manage the impulse instead of living on autopilot.
cards.shortDescription: Anger is a signal, not a verdict. We’ll identify your triggers and patterns to regain control.
questions: Over the last 7 days, what 2–3 situations made you angriest (home/work/road/online)? Write them and rate anger 1–10.
What’s the common thread: boundaries, disrespect, unfairness, chaos, helplessness, exhaustion? What meaning does your brain attach (e.g., “I’m not respected”)?
cards.recommendation:
When you can see triggers, anger stops being “a storm”. You catch it earlier and choose a response.

Do this (1 week, 5 notes):
1) Situation (where/with whom/what happened).
2) Anger 1–10.
3) What was crossed: boundary / respect / fairness / control.
4) First impulse (say, slam, punish, prove, leave).

At the end, look for the main repeating trigger—that’s your “primary button”.
cards.mechanism: Trigger identification reduces impulsivity and supports early regulation.

cards.introduction:
Anger often looks like strength, but underneath it can be vulnerability: shame, hurt, fear, helplessness. Many men were taught it’s safer to be angry than to admit “that hurt”.

When you can spot what’s underneath, your reaction becomes more precise: you protect what matters instead of burning everything down. In this card you’ll practise rewinding one minute back and naming the primary feeling before the spike.
cards.shortDescription: Anger often covers a more vulnerable feeling. We’ll identify what was there a minute before the spike to respond more accurately.
questions: What did you feel a minute before your last anger outburst?
What fear, pain, or helplessness might have been behind this anger?
cards.recommendation:
Naming the primary feeling makes anger a signal—not a commander.

Do this after a spike (3–5 minutes):
1) Fact: what happened.
2) Anger 1–10.
3) One minute earlier: what did I feel (hurt/shame/fear/exhaustion)? Rate 1–10.
4) Need: respect, safety, being heard, rest.
5) One sentence you could say next time instead of exploding.

You don’t have to show vulnerability to everyone—just stop hiding it from yourself.
cards.mechanism: Recognising primary emotions reduces anger intensity and improves self-control.

cards.introduction:
Your body signals anger before you can think: jaw tight, shoulders up, fists tense, breathing short. If you catch those signals early, you get a 10–30 second window to choose what you do next.

Many men miss that window because “no time to feel”. But the body is your early warning system.

In this card you’ll identify your physical markers and build a quick pause protocol.
cards.shortDescription: The body warns you before the outburst. We’ll identify your early physical markers and use a short pause to regain choice.
questions: How do you feel anger in your body? Where does it live: in your chest, jaw, fists?
What physical signals appear before outbursts?
cards.recommendation:
A body-based pause lowers arousal and gives your “thinking brain” control again.

Do this when you notice early signs:
1) Label: “anger is rising”.
2) Drop shoulders, unclench jaw.
3) 3 breathing cycles: inhale 4, exhale 6.
4) Choose one action: clarify, set a boundary, or take a break.

This isn’t weakness. It’s self-management.
cards.mechanism: Longer exhales reduce arousal and interrupt escalation.

cards.introduction:
Irritation is fuel. Sometimes a conflict is needed—to set a boundary, protect something important.

Other times irritation is just exhaustion, hunger, overload, lack of sleep, and fighting will only make it worse. Many men go into battle out of habit instead of checking their capacity.

In this card you’ll learn to distinguish “this needs a conversation” from “my system is overheated” and rehearse an alternative script.
cards.shortDescription: Not every irritation needs a fight. We’ll practise distinguishing “needs solving” from “I’m overheated” and choose a better script.
questions: When did you last regret your outburst?
What could you have done differently in that moment?
cards.recommendation:
Rehearsal gives your brain a plan instead of improvising under anger.

Do this (5 minutes):
1) Write the trigger situation.
2) Note early body signs/thoughts.
3) Write a 3‑step script: pause → sentence → action.
4) Say it out loud once.

Example: Pause + “I’m getting heated; I need 10 minutes” + step away, then return.

The goal isn’t “never angry”—it’s “no regret afterwards”.
cards.mechanism: Rehearsal reduces impulsivity and increases behavioural choice.

cards.introduction:
Anger often flares because of the meaning you assigned: “they’re disrespecting me”, “they’re trying to push me”, “they don’t care”. Sometimes that’s true—sometimes it’s an automatic interpretation.

Many men are especially sensitive to respect/status, so the meaning lights up fast. In this card you’ll practise cognitive flexibility: find other plausible explanations so you can respond strongly but cleanly.
cards.shortDescription: Interpretation can fuel anger more than the fact. We’ll practise alternative meanings to reduce automatic accusations.
questions: What did you think about the person or situation in the moment of anger?
What other explanations could there be for their behavior?
cards.recommendation:
Alternatives don’t cancel boundaries—they reduce reactivity.

In the moment:
1) Notice the blaming thought.
2) Ask: “What else could this mean?” (2 alternatives).
3) Choose: clarify, set a boundary, or postpone.

This is not “excusing”. It’s not swinging blind.
cards.mechanism: Cognitive flexibility reduces anger intensity and escalation risk.

cards.introduction:
Anger often grows where expectations are rigid: “they must”, “they should”, “normally you would”. The more rigid the rule, the faster the flare—especially when you’re tired.

For many men it’s tied to control: if the world doesn’t comply, you want to force it. You can make expectations more flexible without becoming a doormat.

In this card you’ll rewrite one rigid “must” into a preference plus a boundary.
cards.shortDescription: Rigid expectations fuel anger. We’ll rewrite one “must” into a flexible preference + boundary.
questions: What expectation was violated in your last case of irritation?
How realistic was it?
cards.recommendation:
Flexibility reduces triggers. You stay firm on what matters, but stop exploding over the inevitable.

Do this:
1) Write the rigid rule.
2) Rewrite: “It matters to me that X. If it’s not met, I will do Y.”
3) Write one calm sentence you can say out loud.

This is strategy, not surrender.
cards.mechanism: Reframing rigid rules reduces reactivity and supports self-control.

cards.introduction:
Being angry is normal. The question is where the energy goes—destruction or protection of values.

If you’ve learned to “hold it”, it can come out as blow‑ups or cold silence. Either way it damages you and relationships.

You need a safe release channel that doesn’t hurt anyone. In this card you’ll pick one ecological release method and schedule it so anger doesn’t pile up to an explosion.
cards.shortDescription: Anger is energy. We’ll choose a safe way to release it so it doesn’t accumulate into blow-ups.
questions: How do you usually express anger: with words, actions, silence?
What of this helps you, and what destroys relationships or yourself?
cards.recommendation:
Safe release lowers buildup and reduces the chance of an outburst.

Do this (1 week):
1) Choose one method for 10–20 minutes.
2) Schedule it twice (before you’re at the limit).
3) Rate anger 1–10 before/after.

If you ever feel like you might hurt someone, prioritise safety and seek help immediately.
cards.mechanism: Releasing arousal safely reduces impulsive behaviour risk.

cards.introduction:
A common reason for irritation is unspoken boundaries. You tolerate, act like it’s fine, and inside it builds: “I’m being used / ignored / disrespected”.

Then anger fires in the wrong place—at loved ones or small things. Many men tolerate “like a man” until the lid blows.

In this card you’ll pick one spot where you tolerate too long and set a preventive boundary: early, calm, clear.
cards.shortDescription: If you tolerate too long, anger will build. We’ll set one preventive boundary early and calmly.
questions: Where do you most often endure until the last moment — then explode?
How could you gently mark your boundary beforehand?
cards.recommendation:
Preventive boundaries reduce buildup.

Do this:
1) Pick one repeating situation.
2) Write a short boundary sentence (max ~12 words).
3) Say it out loud 3 times.
4) Use it once within 3 days.

Example: “I’m not discussing this in that tone. Calm down or I’ll pause.”
cards.mechanism: Assertive boundaries reduce buildup and reactivity.

cards.introduction:
Understanding “why I’m angry” helps, but change happens when you practise a new behaviour in a familiar trigger situation. Many men respect proof over talk—good.

In this card you’ll pick one common blow‑up scenario and test a new strategy for 3 days. The goal is not “never angry”.

The goal is “strong without damage”.
cards.shortDescription: Change requires practice. We’ll run a 3‑day behavioural experiment in a common anger trigger situation.
questions: In what situation do you most often lose control — and would like to react differently?
How can you test a new behavioral strategy in the next 3 days?
cards.recommendation:
Experiments create new experience and new options.

Do this (3 days):
1) Define the trigger.
2) Pick one new response.
3) Use it at least twice.
4) Note the result each time.

Use an if‑then: “If X starts, I do Y.”
cards.mechanism: Behavioural experiments build new responses and reduce automatic escalation.

cards.introduction:
Sometimes anger is protective energy: for your boundaries, dignity, family, fairness. The problem starts when protection becomes destruction—shouting, humiliation, threats, hitting.

That doesn’t mean you’re “bad”; it means you need a clear code for how you protect what matters. In this card you’ll write a simple “protection code”: what you stand for, what you do when angry, and what you don’t do—so your strength stays safe.
cards.shortDescription: Anger can protect values or destroy relationships. We’ll write your “protection code” so strength stays safe.
questions: What or whom would you like to protect when you're angry?
How can you express this protection more constructively?
cards.recommendation:
Rules reduce impulsivity. You know what you do when you’re heated.

Do this (7 minutes):
1) Write 3 values you protect.
2) Write 3 actions you do when angry (constructive).
3) Write 3 actions you don’t do (what you regret later).
4) Practise one point this week.
cards.mechanism: Clear behavioural rules reduce impulsivity and increase self-control.
